title: AGENTHARM: A BENCHMARK FOR MEASURINGHARMFULNESS OF LLM AGENTS
summary: It introduces Agent Harm, a large-scale, diverse benchmark specifically designed to evaluate the safety and harmfulness of LLM agents across 440 tasks in 11 distinct harm categories. Unlike traditional chatbot evaluations, this benchmark assesses a model's ability to execute complex, multi-step malicious workflows using external tools after a safety refusal is bypassed. The study reveals that many leading LLMs are surprisingly compliant with harmful agentic requests even without explicit jailbreaking. Finally, the authors provide a standardized evaluation framework and a public leaderboard to facilitate the development of more robust defensive measures for autonomous AI agents.

title: AgentDojo: A Dynamic Environment to Evaluate Attacks and Defenses for LLM Agents
summary: The paper introduces **AgentDojo**, a dynamic evaluation framework designed to assess the adversarial robustness of LLM agents against prompt injection attacks. Unlike static test suites, AgentDojo provides an extensible, stateful environment featuring 97 realistic tasks across diverse domains—such as e-banking and travel—and 629 security test cases. The authors utilize this framework to demonstrate that state-of-the-art models often fail to distinguish instructions from untrusted data, incurring significant utility loss or succumbing to malicious tasks when under attack. Ultimately, the work establishes a standardized benchmark to foster the development of more robust agent designs and adaptive defense mechanisms.

title: INJECAGENT: Benchmarking Indirect Prompt Injections in Tool-Integrated Large Language Model Agents
summary: The paper introduces **INJECAGENT**, the first comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate the vulnerability of tool-using LLM agents to indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks. The authors curated a dataset of 1,054 test cases covering 17 user tools and 62 attacker tools across diverse domains like finance and smart home management, categorizing attacks into direct harm and private data exfiltration. By evaluating 30 different LLM agents, the study reveals that most are highly susceptible to these attacks—especially when instructions are reinforced with

—while also demonstrating that fine-tuned agents generally offer greater resilience than those relying solely on prompting. Ultimately, the work highlights critical security gaps in current agent deployments and provides a standardized framework for developing future mitigation strategies.

title: AGENTPOISON: Red-teaming LLM Agents via Poisoning Memory or Knowledge Bases
summary: The paper introduces **AGENTPOISON**, the first backdoor attack framework specifically designed to red-team retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) based LLM agents. The authors propose a **constrained optimization approach** that generates stealthy, cohesive triggers to map malicious instructions into a unique, compact region of the embedding space, ensuring high retrieval rates for poisoned content while preserving the agent's performance on benign queries. Extensive experiments across autonomous driving, healthcare, and QA domains demonstrate that AGENTPOISON achieves an average attack success rate exceeding 80% with a poisoning ratio of less than 0.1%. Furthermore, the findings reveal that these optimized triggers exhibit significant **transferability** across different RAG embedders and resilience against common defensive measures like rephrasing or perplexity filters.

title: Watch Out for Your Agents! Investigating Backdoor Threats to LLM-Based Agents
summary: This paper takes the first step in investigating backdoor threats to LLM-based agents by formulating a general framework for agent backdoor attacks. The authors categorize these attacks into three diverse and covert forms—**Query-Attack**, **Observation-Attack**, and **Thought-Attack**—which can manipulate either the final output distribution or intermediate reasoning steps. Extensive experiments on the AgentInstruct and ToolBench benchmarks demonstrate that these agents are highly vulnerable to such attacks, often achieving high success rates while maintaining normal performance on benign tasks. Finally, the study highlights that existing textual backdoor defense methods are largely ineffective in the agent setting, emphasizing an urgent need for targeted defensive research.

title: AntiFake: Using Adversarial Audio to Prevent Unauthorized Speech Synthesis
summary: The paper introduces **AntiFake**, a proactive defense mechanism designed to prevent unauthorized DeepFake speech synthesis by applying stealthy adversarial perturbations to a user's original audio. Unlike traditional post-attack detection methods, AntiFake disrupts the synthesis process by deviating speaker embeddings, ensuring that any resulting unauthorized audio sounds like a different person to both human listeners and voice authentication systems. To ensure protection against unknown attackers, the system utilizes an **ensemble learning approach** and a **human-in-the-loop workflow** that balances strong security with the preservation of natural speech quality. Experimental results demonstrate that AntiFake is highly effective, achieving over a **95% protection rate** against five state-of-the-art synthesizers, including commercial black-box models.

title: F5-TTS: A Fairytaler that Fakes Fluent and Faithful Speech with Flow Matching
summary: F5-TTS is a non-autoregressive text-to-speech (TTS) system that utilizes flow matching with a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) to achieve high-fidelity, zero-shot speech synthesis. Its core contributions include a simplified architecture that eliminates the need for complex components like phoneme alignment or duration models by using a character-based input refined by ConvNeXt blocks for improved text-speech alignment. Furthermore, the paper introduces an inference-time

strategy that enhances the model's efficiency and performance, enabling a significantly faster real-time factor (RTF) of 0.15 compared to state-of-the-art models. Experimental results validate that F5-TTS achieves superior naturalness and robustness, even when trained on a 100K-hour multilingual dataset and tested against challenging zero-shot and code-switching scenarios.

title: ALIF: Low-Cost Adversarial Audio Attacks on Black-Box Speech Platforms using Linguistic Features
summary: This paper introduces **ALIF**, the first black-box adversarial attack pipeline that leverages linguistic feature space to manipulate speech recognition systems. By generating perturbations directly within the low-dimensional embedding space—where the decision boundary of deep learning models resides—the authors overcome traditional limitations such as high query costs and vulnerability to model updates. The research presents two practical schemes: **ALIF-OTL** for digital attacks and **ALIF-OTA** for physical environments, both of which demonstrate significant query efficiency improvements of up to 97.7% over existing methods while maintaining high success rates against commercial ASR platforms. Ultimately, the study highlights a critical security flaw in voice-controllable devices by proving that attack samples can remain robust and effective even after service providers update their underlying models.

title: Practical Hidden Voice Attacks against Speech and Speaker Recognition Systems
summary: This paper introduces a practical, model-agnostic (black-box) approach for generating hidden voice commands that are correctly transcribed by Voice Processing Systems (VPSes) while remaining unintelligible to humans. By exploiting the signal processing phase common to most VPSes—rather than attacking specific machine learning models—the authors developed four classes of audio perturbations based on psychoacoustic principles. Their evaluation demonstrated that these attacks are highly effective across 12 different machine learning models, including seven proprietary APIs (such as Google and Bing Speech), and remain successful across diverse hardware configurations and real-world acoustic environments. Ultimately, the work proves that domain-specific knowledge of audio signal processing allows for the rapid generation of practical hidden voice attacks that are difficult to counter through traditional model-hardening techniques.

title: The Lies Characters Tell: Utilizing Large Language Models to Normalize Adversarial Unicode Perturbations
summary: This paper explores the use of large language models (LLMs) to neutralize adversarial homoglyphs—Unicode characters that visually mimic Latin letters to bypass content filters—by introducing two novel normalization methods: direct and indirect normalization. The researchers contributed a new dataset of 29,846 homoglyph-augmented tweets and demonstrated that LLM-based approaches, particularly GPT-4o-mini, significantly outperform traditional normalization tools in terms of cosine similarity and Levenshtein distance. Their findings indicate that utilizing LLMs as a pre-processing step for hate speech detection models can effectively restore model performance to near-human-normalized levels without requiring extensive domain-specific training data.

title: Bad Characters:Imperceptible NLP Attacks
summary: This paper introduces a novel class of **imperceptible encoding-based attacks** against natural language processing (NLP) systems by exploiting the discrepancy between how humans perceive text and how machines encode it. The researchers developed four black-box attack variants—using **invisible characters, homoglyphs, reorderings, and deletions**—and demonstrated that these can significantly degrade model performance or cause denial-of-service through

. Their evaluation across eight open-source and commercial models (including those from Google and Microsoft) revealed that nearly all systems are vulnerable to these perturbations, which preserve semantic meaning while remaining invisible to the human eye. Finally, the authors propose several **defense mechanisms**, such as input sanitization and character mapping, while highlighting the inherent complexity of protecting rapidly deploying large-scale NLP systems.

title: IMPERCEPTIBLE JAILBREAKING AGAINST LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
summary: This paper introduces a novel textual jailbreaking method that uses invisible Unicode characters, specifically **variation selectors**, to bypass the safety alignments of Large Language Models (LLMs). By appending these invisible characters as adversarial suffixes to malicious questions, the authors create jailbreak prompts that appear visually identical to the original questions on screen while

altering their tokenization to induce harmful responses. To optimize these suffixes, the study proposes a **chain-of-search pipeline** that iteratively refines the characters to maximize the likelihood of target-start tokens like

. Experimental results demonstrate that this method achieves high attack success rates against four aligned LLMs and successfully generalizes to prompt injection attacks, all while remaining completely imperceptible to human users.

title: Jailbreaking Black Box Large Language Models in Twenty Queries
summary: The paper introduces **Prompt Automatic Iterative Refinement (PAIR)**, an algorithm that automates the generation of semantic, human-interpretable jailbreaks for large language models (LLMs). By pitting an
LLM against a

LLM, PAIR iteratively refines candidate prompts based on feedback to bypass safety guardrails without requiring internal model access. This approach is significantly more efficient than existing token-level attacks, often discovering successful jailbreaks in fewer than twenty queries—a 250-fold improvement over prior methods. Ultimately, the authors demonstrate PAIR's high effectiveness and transferability across both open- and closed-source models, including GPT-4 and Gemini-Pro, highlighting critical vulnerabilities in current AI alignment strategies.

title: BEYOND BINARY REWARDS: TRAINING LMS TO REASON ABOUT THEIR UNCERTAINTY
summary: This paper introduces **Reinforcement Learning with Calibration Rewards (RLCR)**, a training approach that enables language models to generate both reasoning chains and accurate numerical confidence estimates for their predictions. By augmenting standard binary correctness rewards with a **Brier score**—a proper scoring rule for uncertainty—the authors prove and empirically demonstrate that models can be incentivized to be both accurate and well-calibrated. Experimental results across diverse factual and mathematical tasks show that RLCR substantially reduces overconfident

and improves calibration on out-of-distribution data without sacrificing task performance. Furthermore, the study demonstrates that these verbalized confidence scores can be effectively leveraged at test-time to improve model reliability through confidence-weighted scaling methods.

title: Automatic and Universal Prompt Injection Attacks against Large Language Models
summary: The paper introduces a unified framework that categorizes prompt injection objectives into static, semi-dynamic, and dynamic goals. To address these threats, the authors present a momentum-enhanced gradient-based search algorithm that automatically generates highly effective and universal injection data. Their research demonstrates that this method can achieve a 50% average attack success rate with as few as five training samples and remains potent even when tested against multiple existing defense mechanisms. Ultimately, the study highlights a critical need for gradient-based robustness testing to prevent the overestimation of LLM security.

title: JudgeDeceiver: Optimizing Prompt Injection Attacks against LLM-as-a-Judge Systems
summary: The paper introduces **JudgeDeceiver**, a novel optimization-based prompt injection attack specifically designed to compromise the integrity of **LLM-as-a-Judge** systems. Unlike traditional handcrafted attacks, this method automates the generation of adversarial sequences by optimizing a multi-component loss function that balances target alignment, token enhancement, and stealthiness to evade detection. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that JudgeDeceiver consistently achieves high attack success rates against popular models like Mistral and OpenChat-3.5 across major benchmarks, effectively bypassing positional biases. Ultimately, the research highlights critical vulnerabilities in automated evaluative frameworks and provides a foundation for developing future security defenses in LLM-based judgment systems.
